title: Meet the Technology Pioneers driving innovation in 2026
gist: WEF's 2026 Technology Pioneers cohort (100 companies, 23 countries) maps five structural shifts in frontier tech: a new AI-infrastructure/agent-economy layer, energy-compute convergence, thematic deep-tech clusters, deep tech's geographic spread beyond Silicon Valley, and two dominant founder archetypes. AI now accounts for more than 60% of all global venture capital.

insights.claim: AI now accounts for more than 60% of all global venture capital, with deep tech investment surging and the lines between software, hardware, energy and biology increasingly blurred.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Capital is crowding into AI to a degree that distorts the rest of the VC landscape; non-AI startups face a starved funding environment, and AI exposure is effectively the dominant VC beta.
insights.quote: deep tech investment is surging, AI accounts for more than 60% of all global venture capital, and the lines between software, hardware, energy and biology are increasingly blurred.
insights.locator: Every year, the Forum selects 100 early-stage companies

insights.claim: AI itself is now lowering the capital and headcount needed to attempt hard deep-tech problems (fusion, lunar extraction, neurosurgical robotics), enabling small early-stage teams to attack challenges that previously required enormous budgets.
insights.speaker: Verena Kuhn
insights.so_what: Lower cost-to-attempt compresses the timeline and capital intensity of moonshot tech, widening the funnel of credible deep-tech bets investors can underwrite.
insights.quote: they're working on challenges that until recently would have required enormous budgets, infrastructure and headcount

insights.claim: AI venture funding is rotating from application-layer wrappers toward startups building infrastructure for an 'agent economy' (identity/payments, billing, GPU orchestration, world models), with eight 2026 cohort companies in this category that did not exist as commercial products three years ago.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: The value-capture layer in AI is shifting down-stack to agent infrastructure; watch picks-and-shovels for autonomous agents (identity, payments, orchestration) as the next funding wave.
insights.quote: rising funding for start-ups building the infrastructure to support an agent economy. These start-ups are forming with notable speed, signalling where value can be created in AI.
insights.locator: 1. The arrival of an AI infrastructure layer

insights.claim: Inception is building diffusion-based large language models that it says run several times faster than pattern-recognizing autoregressive architectures, signalling a credible challenge to the dominant autoregressive LLM design.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: A viable diffusion-LLM architecture would reset inference-cost economics and threaten the autoregressive incumbency assumed across the AI compute stack.
insights.quote: Inception, which builds diffusion-based large language models that the company says run several times faster than pattern-recognizing autoregressive architectures
insights.locator: 1. The arrival of an AI infrastructure layer

insights.claim: Data centres are expected to consume twice as much power by 2030 due to surging AI demand, and there is currently no dominant solution among the contenders (geothermal, fusion, space-based solar, grid orchestration).
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Power, not chips, is becoming the binding constraint on AI scaling; the absence of a dominant solution keeps multiple energy bets live for investors and signals grid-capacity arbitrage opportunities.
insights.quote: Data centres are expected to consume twice as much power by 2030 due to surging AI demand.
insights.locator: 2. The convergence of energy and compute

insights.claim: Grid orchestration startups (Emerald AI, GridCARE) are helping data-centre developers get online faster by unlocking 'hidden' capacity on existing grids, positioning capacity-forecasting software as a near-term bottleneck-reliever ahead of new generation.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Software that frees stranded grid capacity is a faster, cheaper lever than new build-out; a monetizable wedge while fusion/geothermal mature.
insights.quote: helping facilities get online by unlocking 'hidden' capacity on existing grids
insights.locator: 2. The convergence of energy and compute

insights.claim: Venture capital into physical AI and robotics reached $26 billion last year, up from $4.2 billion in 2019 — a roughly 6x increase over five years as systems that perceive, reason and act in the physical world gain prominence.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Physical AI is now a major VC vertical in its own right; the steep funding ramp flags it as a leading edge for industrial automation and humanoid exposure.
insights.quote: $26 billion in venture capital went into the space last year, compared to $4.2 billion in 2019
insights.locator: 3. The emergence of thematic tech clusters

insights.claim: Quantum computing is transitioning from laboratory to commercial infrastructure, with growing cohort representation reflecting long-term bets by governments and enterprises on computational advantage and quantum-resistant security.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Quantum-resistant security is becoming a procurement theme as governments hedge; an early commercialization signal for post-quantum cryptography vendors.
insights.locator: 3. The emergence of thematic tech clusters

insights.claim: Bioeconomy startups are commercializing biology as a manufacturing platform — using fermentation and synthetic biology instead of agriculture or petrochemicals — to offer industrial alternatives for food, materials and chemicals.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Synthetic-biology manufacturing is a long-horizon substitution threat to agriculture and petrochemical supply chains; watch for cost-parity inflection.
insights.locator: 3. The emergence of thematic tech clusters

insights.claim: India's deep-tech and space startups attracted $1.6 billion in venture capital in 2025, a 78% increase from 2023, and India contributes nine companies (mostly space and deep tech) to the 2026 cohort.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: India is emerging as a credible deep-tech and space hub with fast-rising funding; a geographic diversification signal away from Silicon Valley concentration.
insights.quote: the country's start-ups attracted $1.6 billion in venture capital funding in 2025, a 78% increase from 2023.
insights.locator: 4. Deep tech's spread beyond Silicon Valley

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia's Intella is building Arabic-first speech AI for 400 million speakers, addressing a critically underserved language-technology gap, while Colombia's Quipu uses AI credit assessment to bring the informal workforce into conventional finance.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Localized, language- and inclusion-specific AI in emerging markets is a whitespace incumbents underserve; large addressable populations support standalone regional champions.
insights.locator: 4. Deep tech's spread beyond Silicon Valley

insights.claim: The 2026 cohort concentrates into two founder archetypes — the scientist-founder with decades in the lab, and the 'repeat builder' infrastructure founder who already exited the layer beneath their current company (e.g. GridCARE's Amit Narayan, RLWRLD's Junghee Ryu who sold Olaworks to Intel).
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: Deep-tech founder quality is bifurcating toward lab veterans and serial infra builders; a useful diligence screen for which frontier bets have credible execution.
insights.locator: 5. The prominence of two founder archetypes

insights.claim: Frontier-tech innovation is becoming systems-based — gated not just by ideas but by access to power, compute, supply chains and critical minerals — meaning breakthroughs increasingly require partnerships and ecosystems to reach scale.
insights.speaker: Michelle Mormont
insights.so_what: The bottleneck to scaling deep tech is shifting from invention to physical inputs and coordination; value accrues to whoever controls power, compute and minerals access.
insights.quote: innovation in frontier tech is increasingly systems-based and shaped by not just ideas but factors including access to power, compute, supply chains and more.
